past_medical_history: Polycystic ovary syndrome diagnosed at age 22
family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus, rheumatoid arthritis
No family history of epilepsy or psychiatric illness

social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies current use; remote history of marijuana in college
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a suburban home

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but confused and intermittently agitated. Oriented to self only; unable to state the date, month, or location. Speech is fluent but tangential with occasional paraphasic errors. Able to follow simple one-step commands but fails multi-step instructions. Intermittent pressured speech alternating with periods of decreased verbal output. Short-term memory significantly impaired: recalls 1 of 3 objects at 5 minutes. Attention is poor; unable to spell WORLD backwards or perform serial 7s. Intermittent paranoid ideation, stating the nursing staff is trying to harm her.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm, equal and reactive bilaterally. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact. Subtle decreased nasolabial fold on the left, though cooperation limits reliable assessment. Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations. Intermittent perioral dyskinesias with repetitive lip movements noted during examination.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone mildly increased in bilateral upper extremities, more pronounced on the right. Strength grossly 4+/5 throughout, limited by poor cooperation and fluctuating attention. No definite focal weakness. Occasional stereotyped hand movements bilaterally, described as pill-rolling and finger-wiggling.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to pain symmetrically in all extremities. Detailed sensory testing unreliable due to encephalopathy and poor cooperation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk), Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing performed inconsistently; mild bilateral dysmetria noted but reliability is questionable given encephalopathy. Heel-to-shin not assessed due to inability to cooperate.
neurological_exam.gait: Unsteady, wide-based gait with tendency to veer to the right. Required two-person assist. Tandem gait not attempted.
neurological_exam.additional: Intermittent perioral dyskinesias and stereotyped hand movements observed during examination. One episode of sudden behavioral arrest lasting approximately 10 seconds with staring and lip automatisms, followed by confusion, witnessed during the encounter. Heart rate noted to fluctuate between 85 and 118 bpm during the 45-minute evaluation without clear precipitant.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion, personality change, insomnia, and a witnessed seizure over three weeks
patient.history_present_illness: This 33-year-old right-handed woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband after three weeks of progressive behavioral and cognitive decline, culminating in a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure earlier today. Her husband reports that approximately three weeks ago she began having difficulty sleeping, waking multiple times per night and sometimes not sleeping at all. He initially attributed this to work stress as she had recently taken on additional responsibilities at her school. Within a few days, she became increasingly irritable and emotionally labile, crying without clear provocation and snapping at her students, which was highly unusual for her. She saw her primary care physician ten days ago, who attributed the symptoms to worsening anxiety and increased her sertraline from 50 mg to 100 mg daily. Despite this, her symptoms progressed. Over the following week, she became increasingly confused, forgetting conversations from minutes earlier and misplacing household items. She began expressing paranoid thoughts, telling her husband that her coworkers were conspiring against her and that hidden cameras were placed in their home. Her husband took her to an urgent psychiatric evaluation five days ago, where she was prescribed olanzapine 5 mg nightly for possible first-episode psychosis. However, her mental status continued to deteriorate. Three days ago, her husband noticed involuntary repetitive lip movements and odd hand gestures that she did not seem aware of. Yesterday, she was unable to prepare a simple meal and appeared disoriented within their home. This morning, her husband witnessed her develop sudden bilateral arm stiffening followed by generalized shaking lasting approximately 60 seconds with tongue biting and urinary incontinence, followed by 20 minutes of post-ictal confusion. Emergency medical services were called and she was transported to the emergency department. Upon arrival, she was intermittently agitated and confused, unable to provide a coherent history. She has had no recent travel, no known sick contacts, no tick bites, and no new medications other than those prescribed in the past two weeks. Her husband denies any recreational drug use.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly formed, intermittent 7 Hz theta activity over the occipital regions

findings.type: Intermittent rhythmic delta activity with superimposed faster frequencies
findings.frequency: 1.5-2 Hz delta with intermittent superimposed 15-20 Hz beta
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta with occasional overriding beta activity, not meeting full criteria for extreme delta brush
Evolving rhythmic theta activity for 12 seconds with abrupt offset

artifacts.description: Intermittent muscle artifact from perioral dyskinesias and jaw movements

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient's inability to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG showing moderate diffuse background slowing, generalized rhythmic delta activity, intermittent delta-beta pattern not meeting full criteria for extreme delta brush, and one brief electrographic seizure of left temporal origin. The findings indicate diffuse cerebral dysfunction with epileptogenic activity. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 45 minutes. Significant muscle artifact during portions of the study. Continuous EEG monitoring recommended.

findings.location: Left medial temporal lobe, involving the hippocampus and amygdala

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity, best appreciated on coronal sequences

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
No additional white matter lesions to suggest demyelinating disease
Contralateral right temporal lobe appears normal
mri.impression: Subtle unilateral left medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity involving the hippocampus and amygdala, without restricted diffusion, enhancement, or mass effect. The unilateral distribution and absence of hemorrhagic changes are atypical for HSV encephalitis but do not exclude it. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Chloride 108 mEq/L (H), Creatinine 0.5 mg/dL (L), CRP 0.9 mg/dL (H), CK 200 U/L (H), Prolactin 44 ng/mL (H).

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Equivocal (2 faint bands, unclear if present in serum)
special_tests.Cytology: Rare reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 19 cmH2O. WBC: 12 cells/uL (mildly elevated) (8% PMN/88% lymph). Protein: 57.2 mg/dL. Glucose: 56.8 mg/dL (ratio 0.61 (serum glucose 102 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Equivocal (2 faint bands, unclear if present in serum). IgG index: 0.72 (borderline elevated, normal <0.70). HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. VZV PCR: Negative. Enterovirus PCR: Negative. Bacterial culture: No growth at 48 hours. Gram stain: No organisms seen. Cytology: Rare reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:80)  (H), CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:128)  (H).

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 3.5 x 2.8 x 2.6 cm with multiple peripheral follicles consistent with polycystic morphology. No discrete mass.
4.1 x 3.0 x 2.9 cm, polycystic morphology. A 1.2 cm hypoechoic area is noted, likely a dominant follicle or simple cyst. No solid component or calcification.
Normal size, 7.8 x 4.2 x 3.9 cm. Endometrial stripe 8 mm, normal for reproductive age.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor Ab (CSF) Positive (1:128)  (H), ANA 1:80 speckled  (H).

additional_observations: Bilateral polycystic ovaries without discrete mass or teratoma
Small left renal cyst (0.6 cm), likely incidental
output.impression: No evidence of malignancy, teratoma, or lymphadenopathy. Incidental small left renal cyst. No paraneoplastic tumor identified. In the absence of a tumor trigger, the patient's anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is classified as non-paraneoplastic. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, not present in matched serum sample)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 19 cmH2O. WBC: 20 cells/uL (elevated) (5% PMN/92% lymph). Protein: 60.9 mg/dL. Glucose: 55.4 mg/dL (ratio 0.60 (serum glucose 100 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, not present in matched serum sample). IgG index: 0.78 (elevated). Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:128). Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells.
Abnormal values: Olanzapine assessment Olanzapine 5 mg nightly - currently prescribed  (H).

results.title: A clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis
results.authors: Graus F, Titulaer MJ, Balu R, et al.
results.key_finding: Proposed diagnostic criteria for autoimmune encephalitis. Probable anti-NMDAR encephalitis can be diagnosed with rapid onset of at least four of: abnormal psychiatric behavior, speech dysfunction, seizures, movement disorder, decreased consciousness, or autonomic dysfunction, plus at least one of abnormal EEG, CSF pleocytosis, or MRI findings suggestive of encephalitis.

results.title: Treatment and prognostic factors for long-term outcome in patients with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
results.authors: Titulaer MJ, McCracken L, Gabilondo I, et al.
results.key_finding: In 577 patients, 81% achieved good outcome (mRS 0-2) at 24 months. Early treatment was the strongest predictor of good outcome. First-line therapy (steroids, IVIG, plasma exchange) led to improvement in 53%. Second-line therapy (rituximab, cyclophosphamide) was needed in 47% and led to additional improvement in 65% of those patients.

results.title: Frequency and syndrome of anti-NMDAR encephalitis without detectable tumor
results.authors: Florance NR, Davis RL, Lam C, et al.
results.key_finding: Approximately 40-50% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases have no identifiable tumor. Non-paraneoplastic cases have similar clinical features but may have a higher relapse rate. Continued surveillance imaging is recommended.

output.summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is treatable with immunotherapy, with 81% achieving good outcomes at 24 months when treated early. First-line therapy includes corticosteroids, IVIG, or plasma exchange. Approximately half of cases lack an identifiable tumor. Diagnostic criteria require rapid onset of neuropsychiatric symptoms plus supportive paraclinical findings.

differential.key_distinguishing: Pre-existing anxiety diagnosis and initial psychiatric presentation may bias toward this diagnosis. However, the subsequent development of seizures, perioral dyskinesias, autonomic lability, CSF pleocytosis, and abnormal EEG are not consistent with primary psychiatric illness.

Temporal lobe involvement on MRI raises concern, but MRI lacks hemorrhagic changes and restricted diffusion typical of HSV. CSF HSV PCR is negative. Clinical course is subacute rather than hyperacute.

Recent olanzapine exposure makes this a consideration, but onset of psychiatric and neurological symptoms preceded antipsychotic use. CK is only mildly elevated (210, not >1000). Temperature is only mildly elevated. Symptoms are progressive rather than temporally related to drug initiation.
Family history of autoimmune disease is notable, but normal TSH, free T4, and anti-TPO antibodies effectively exclude this diagnosis.
Would expect multifocal ischemic lesions on MRI and vessel irregularities on angiography. The clinical picture of movement disorder and psychiatric symptoms is atypical for vasculitis.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: IV access, continuous cardiac and pulse oximetry monitoring, seizure precautions. Administer IV lorazepam 2 mg if seizure recurs.
Obtain urgent labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, ESR, CK, liver function, thyroid function, urine drug screen, prolactin level
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia, elevated CK from seizure, elevated prolactin, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, normal thyroid function, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Review medication list and note recent olanzapine initiation; consider discontinuation given clinical picture
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification that neuropsychiatric symptoms preceded antipsychotic use, arguing against NMS

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle left medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity; nonspecific but consistent with early encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, viral PCRs, oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, borderline elevated protein, equivocal oligoclonal bands, anti-NMDA receptor antibodies pending

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric treatment for possible HSV encephalitis until PCR results return negative
Diffuse slowing, intermittent delta-beta activity suggestive of but not diagnostic for anti-NMDAR encephalitis, electrographic seizure

optimal_actions.action: Start anti-seizure medication: levetiracetam 1000 mg IV loading dose then 500 mg IV q12h
Given high clinical suspicion for autoimmune encephalitis despite pending antibodies, initiate empiric first-line immunotherapy: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initiation of immunotherapy should not be delayed for antibody results when clinical suspicion is high

optimal_actions.action: Add IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days concurrently with steroids, or consider plasma exchange as alternative
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Combination first-line immunotherapy for maximal efficacy

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Polycystic ovaries consistent with PCOS but no teratoma identified in this case

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue olanzapine; use lorazepam 1-2 mg IV q4-6h as needed for agitation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Safer agitation management with benzodiazepines rather than antipsychotics in this setting

optimal_actions.action: If insufficient response to first-line therapy after 2 weeks, escalate to rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 doses
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Second-line immunotherapy if clinical plateau or deterioration

optimal_actions.action: Fluid restriction to 1.5 L/day for mild SIADH with serial sodium monitoring every 6-8 hours

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Recognize that psychiatric symptoms followed by seizures and movement disorder should prompt consideration of autoimmune encephalitis, not just primary psychiatric illness
Do not delay immunotherapy while waiting for antibody results when clinical suspicion is high
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
Initiate anti-seizure medication for seizure control
Obtain continuous EEG monitoring to assess for subclinical seizures
Discontinue or avoid escalation of antipsychotic medication
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder and discharging with psychiatric medications alone
Escalating antipsychotic dose, which can worsen dyskinesias and precipitate NMS-like reactions in anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Delaying lumbar puncture in a patient with encephalopathy, seizures, and movement disorder
Delaying immunotherapy until antibody results return (5-7 day turnaround) when clinical suspicion is strong
Performing brain biopsy, which is unnecessary and invasive
Rapid sodium correction risking osmotic demyelination
key_reasoning_points: The initial psychiatric presentation with subsequent development of seizures, movement disorder (perioral dyskinesias), and autonomic instability is the hallmark progression of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
Prior treatment as psychiatric illness is a common pitfall; the key red flags are seizures, dyskinesias, and autonomic lability, which are not features of primary psychiatric disease
The EEG shows suggestive but not pathognomonic findings at this stage; the absence of extreme delta brush does not rule out anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Subtle unilateral MRI findings can be seen in early disease or dismissed as post-ictal change; bilateral temporal involvement develops later in many cases
CSF may show only mild pleocytosis early in the course; a low cell count does not exclude autoimmune encephalitis
Anti-NMDA receptor antibody results take days to return; empiric immunotherapy should be started based on clinical suspicion
The absence of an ovarian teratoma occurs in approximately 50% of cases and does not exclude the diagnosis
Family history of autoimmune disease (SLE, RA) may indicate genetic autoimmune predisposition relevant to pathogenesis
Olanzapine use in anti-NMDAR encephalitis is potentially harmful and should be discontinued in favor of benzodiazepines for agitation

moderate_features: Subtle EEG findings without pathognomonic extreme delta brush
Unilateral MRI findings that could be post-ictal
Mild CSF pleocytosis with pending antibody results
